<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh_min_nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh_min_nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8010918]] Wikidata name: Koxinga's Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]] }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in [[:en:Tainan|Tainan]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]].
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against [[:en:Transition from Ming to Qing|Qing forces]]. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]] in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese government]] and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese occupation of Taiwan]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger [[:en:Cultural assimilation|assimilation]] campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant [[:en:Japanization|Japanization]] efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in [[:en:Shinchiku Prefecture|Shinchiku Prefecture]], made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the [[:en:Kuomintang|Kuomintang]] government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the [[:en:Taiwan Strait|Taiwan Strait]]<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by [[:en:Chiang Kai-shek|Chiang Kai-shek]].<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] and many other [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrines]] in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday [[:en:July 14|July 14]] at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />

But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese government]] and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese occupation of Taiwan]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger [[:en:Cultural assimilation|assimilation]] campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant [[:en:Japanization|Japanization]] efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in [[:en:Shinchiku Prefecture|Shinchiku Prefecture]], made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the [[:en:Kuomintang|Kuomintang]] government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the [[:en:Taiwan Strait|Taiwan Strait]]<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by [[:en:Chiang Kai-shek|Chiang Kai-shek]].<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] and many other [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrines]] in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday [[:en:July 14|July 14]] at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />
== See also == * [[Koxinga Ancestral Shrine]] * [[Tainan Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8010918]] Wikidata name: Koxinga's Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]] }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in [[:en:Tainan|Tainan]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]].
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against [[:en:Transition from Ming to Qing|Qing forces]]. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]] in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese government]] and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese occupation of Taiwan]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger [[:en:Cultural assimilation|assimilation]] campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant [[:en:Japanization|Japanization]] efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in [[:en:Shinchiku Prefecture|Shinchiku Prefecture]], made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the [[:en:Kuomintang|Kuomintang]] government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the [[:en:Taiwan Strait|Taiwan Strait]]<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by [[:en:Chiang Kai-shek|Chiang Kai-shek]].<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] and many other [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrines]] in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>

It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.

But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>

Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}

In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8010918]] Wikidata name: Koxinga's Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}} }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against {{ill|Transition from Ming to Qing|en|Transition from Ming to Qing|WD=Q3427966|lt=Qing forces}}. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}} in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese government}} and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese occupation of Taiwan}}.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}} at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />
== See also == * [[Koxinga Ancestral Shrine]] * [[Tainan Shrine]]

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}} at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]]
{{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}} }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against {{ill|Transition from Ming to Qing|en|Transition from Ming to Qing|WD=Q3427966|lt=Qing forces}}. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}} in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese government}} and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese occupation of Taiwan}}.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}} }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.

After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}} in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]]
{{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}} }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against {{ill|Transition from Ming to Qing|en|Transition from Ming to Qing|WD=Q3427966|lt=Qing forces}}. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian}} in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese government}} and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese occupation of Taiwan}}.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}} at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />
== See also == * [[Koxinga Ancestral Shrine]] * [[Tainan Shrine]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}} at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]]
{{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}} }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan|1=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against {{ill|Transition from Ming to Qing|en|Transition from Ming to Qing|WD=Q3427966|lt=Qing forces|1=Transition from Ming to Qing}}. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian|1=Fujian}} in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese government|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}} and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese occupation of Taiwan|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation|1=Cultural assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization|1=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture|1=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang|1=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait|1=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek|1=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14|1=July 14}} at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Koxinga's Shrine]][[en:Koxinga Shrine]][[ja:延平郡王祠]][[ko:연평군왕사]][[zh-min-nan:Iân-pêng-kūn-ông-sû]][[zh:延平郡王祠]]
{{Short description|Temple in Taiwan, formerly a Shinto Shrine}} {{distinguish|Koxinga Ancestral Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:2021st year 的延平郡王祠.jpg | deity = {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}} }} '''Koxinga Shrine''' ({{zh|t=延平郡王祠}}) is a Taiwanese historical temple originally known as '''Kaishan Temple''' ({{zh|t=開山王廟}}). It is located in {{ill|Tainan|en|Tainan|WD=Q140631|lt=Tainan|1=Tainan}}, {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan}}.
==History== Historical records show that a temple was originally built in the 17th century as a Chinese-style [[ancestral shrine]] in honor of Zheng's father {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}.<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2016-10-02 |title=Koxinga Shrine in Taiwan |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2016/10/02/koxinga-shrine-in-taiwan/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau |first=Tainan City Government |date=2017-11-01 |title=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government |url=http://historic.tainan.gov.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=en&task=pageinfo&id=207&index=6 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Koxinga Shrine(延平郡王祠)-Cultural Affairs Bureau, Tainan City Government}}</ref> However, other theories suggest it could be a temple worshipping [[:en:Wang Ye worship|Wang Ye]] or other folk heroes.
Koxinga was also known as a rebel against {{ill|Transition from Ming to Qing|en|Transition from Ming to Qing|WD=Q3427966|lt=Qing forces|1=Transition from Ming to Qing}}. After Qing took control of Taiwan, government moved his grave to {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian|1=Fujian}} in 1699 and banned Koxinga worship. But the people of Taiwan continued to worship him secretly under the name "Prince Zhu".<ref name="crook-2019a">{{Cite book |last=Crook |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jtaPDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA209 |title=Taiwan |date=2019-06-05 |publisher=Bradt Travel Guides |isbn=978-1-78477-622-0 |language=en}}</ref>
In 1897, the shrine was converted into a [[List_of_Shinto_shrines_in_Taiwan|Shinto shrine]] by the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese government|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}} and renamed '''Kaizan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|開山神社|translit=Kaizan Jinja}}) by the Imperial government during the {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese occupation of Taiwan|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}.<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|107}} The Japanese were perceived as attempting to downplay Koxinga's historical reputation and legacy as a folk hero in order to legitimize their rule of Taiwan.<ref name="shei-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Shei |first=Chris |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZyQSEAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA163 |title=Taiwan: Manipulation of Ideology and Struggle for Identity |date=2021-03-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-04783-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Damm |first1=Jens |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AOTJDwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA14 |title=Intercultural Dialogue across Borders: China between Tradition and Modernity |last2=Ne11ermann |first2=Hauke |date=2020 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |isbn=978-3-643-91254-1 |language=en}}</ref> The conversion of the shrine was part of a larger {{ill|Cultural assimilation|en|Cultural assimilation|WD=Q193579|lt=assimilation|1=Cultural assimilation}} campaign to advance idea that Taiwan was always separate from China.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tzeng |first=Shih-jung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=suFxCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA170 |title=From Honto Jin to Bensheng Ren: The Origin and Development of Taiwanese National Consciousness |date=2009-05-16 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-7618-4472-3 |language=en}}</ref>
Kaizan Jinja was uniquely a pre-existing shrine-mausoleum that became recognized as a Shinto shrine. Despite significant {{ill|Japanization|en|Japanization|WD=Q17991037|lt=Japanization|1=Japanization}} efforts, the shrine maintained many Taiwanese elements. For instance, during major festivals, local-style theatre and martial arts were performed. Footage from the 1930s showcases a blend of Japanese and Taiwanese customs during celebrations at the shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Ming dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.
In 1942, Miyazaki Naokatsu, governor of Chūreki District in {{ill|Shinchiku Prefecture|en|Shinchiku Prefecture|WD=Q697879|lt=Shinchiku Prefecture|1=Shinchiku Prefecture}}, made the argument that Taiwanese mausoleums were Shinto, and when Taiwan became Japanese territory, Taiwanese ''[[kami]]'' became Japanese ''kami''..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|108}}
Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of Koxinga, a few blocks away from Koxinga Shrine.<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the three pioneer kami too..<ref name="shimizu-2022a" />{{rp|112}}
In the 1960s, the shrine was converted back to a Confucian one.<ref name="crook-2019a" /> Similarly to the Japanese campaign, the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang|1=Kuomintang}} government used the shrine as a way to legitimize its rule against the ascendant [[:en:People's Republic of China|People's Republic of China]] across the {{ill|Taiwan Strait|en|Taiwan Strait|WD=Q127031|lt=Taiwan Strait|1=Taiwan Strait}}<ref name="harrison-2016a">{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZV3NCwAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA105 |title=Legitimacy, Meaning and Knowledge in the Making of Taiwanese Identity |date=2016-03-15 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-0-230-60169-7 |language=en}}</ref> and was visited by {{ill|Chiang Kai-shek|en|Chiang Kai-shek|WD=Q16574|lt=Chiang Kai-shek|1=Chiang Kai-shek}}.<ref name="harrison-2016a" />
It shares this status with other converted Shinto shrines such as the {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} and many other {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} in Taiwan.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pan |first=Lu |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1rYREAAAQBAJ&dq=Koxinga+Shrine&pg=PA388 |title=image, Imagination and Imaginarium: Remapping World War II Monuments in Greater China |date=2021-01-02 |publisher=Springer Nature |isbn=978-981-15-9674-2 |language=en}}</ref>
Today, there is an annual festival on Koxinga's birthday {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14|1=July 14}} at the shrine.<ref name="shei-2021a" />
== See also == * [[Koxinga Ancestral Shrine]] * [[Tainan Shrine]]
